title: Cartoon lab scientist
text: A readable cartoon lab scientist usually needs a large simple head, clear goggles or glasses, a white coat shape, and one or two lab props. Use bold outlines, simple filled shapes, and a friendly facial expression. Keep the silhouette readable before adding small details.

title: Round laboratory flask
text: A round flask reads well as a circular or pear-shaped body with a narrow neck, translucent blue-green fill, a dark outline, and a few bubbles. Liquid should occupy the lower half so the container remains legible.

title: Full cat vector recipe
text: A full cat should be recognizable without a label: use a rounded body, separate head, two triangular ears, two eyes, small nose and mouth, three whiskers on each side, a curved tail, and four visible legs or paws. Keep the head and ears prominent because they define the silhouette, then add tail and paws so it reads as a complete animal.

title: Padlock vector recipe
text: A padlock reads as a large solid body with a U-shaped shackle above it, visible shackle pins or sockets at the top of the body, and a centered keyhole made from a round top and narrow vertical slot. Highlights and edge strokes help separate the metal shackle from the lock body.

title: House key vector recipe
text: A simple key needs a large circular bow or ring with an inner hole, a long straight shaft, a shoulder or collar near the bow, and several distinct teeth or bit protrusions at the far end. Add a ridge or groove along the shaft so the key has mechanical detail instead of looking like a plain bar.

title: Publication cartoon vector style
text: For publication-quality cartoon vector figures, prefer a limited palette, consistent stroke widths, clean named layers, and large simple forms. Avoid tiny decorative details that disappear when printed. Use labels only where they add scientific meaning.

title: Scientific figure readability
text: A publication figure should remain readable at final column size. Use high contrast between text and background, keep text large enough after scaling, and export vector formats such as PDF or SVG when possible. Check that important objects are not hidden by labels.

title: Illustrator ExtendScript vector drawing
text: Illustrator ExtendScript can create documents, layers, path items, ellipses, rectangles, polygons, lines, text frames, fills, strokes, opacity, names, and exports. Keep generated scripts simple and compatible with the older ExtendScript runtime.

title: Semantic-first image planning
text: Before drawing, retrieve object semantics and publication constraints, then turn those into a structured scene plan. The drawing command should be concrete and validated; retrieved text should inform the plan rather than being executed directly.

title: Molecular self-assembly
text: Molecular self-assembly is clearest as small building blocks organizing into a larger ordered structure. Show monomers as repeated nodes, weak interactions as dotted or pale connector lines, and the final structure as a clustered network or ordered fiber. Use arrows from disordered particles to an organized motif.

title: Catalytic reaction cycle
text: A catalytic mechanism reads well as a surface or active site that binds reactants, lowers an energy barrier, and releases product. Use a solid catalyst slab, highlighted active site, incoming reactant molecules, outgoing product molecules, and an energy landscape inset with a lower catalyzed path.

title: Cell membrane transport
text: A membrane transport concept needs a bilayer barrier, embedded protein channel, concentration gradient, and directional arrows. Use two rows of lipid heads with tails between them, a contrasting channel shape, particles on both sides, and arrows that show selective movement.

title: Electron transfer and charge flow
text: Electron transfer is readable as donor and acceptor sites connected by a bright directional path. Show source and sink nodes, electron dots, a curved transfer arrow, and a small energy-level diagram. Use color contrast to distinguish reduced and oxidized states.

title: Phase separation and domains
text: Phase separation can be shown as mixed particles separating into dense droplets or domains. Use semi-transparent droplets, clustered particles inside, sparse particles outside, and an interface outline. A before-to-after arrow helps explain the transition.

title: Biobased material cycle
text: A biobased material cycle should show renewable feedstock converting into functional material and then returning through reuse or biodegradation. Use plant or biomass icons, reaction/processing arrows, polymer material blocks, product use, and a circular arrow path to emphasize system flow.

title: Abstract mechanism as a map
text: For conceptual science, use a map-like layout: context on the left, mechanism in the center, outcome on the right, and feedback or regulation as curved arrows. Keep each object group visually distinct and label only the conceptual roles, not every decoration.

title: Multiscale zoom hierarchy
text: A multiscale concept is effective as nested zoom panels: macroscale object, mesoscale structure, and molecular detail. Use connecting lines or magnifier rings, and make the smallest scale visually simpler rather than cramming all chemistry into one panel.

title: Scientific concept figure legibility
text: A complex scientific concept figure should still communicate one central mechanism. Separate context, mechanism, and outcome; use consistent arrow language; avoid overlapping labels; and keep every repeated symbol visually consistent across the scene.

title: Illustrator Bezier path drawing
text: The bridge can emit Illustrator path items with anchor points, left and right Bezier handles, smooth or corner point types, open paths, closed paths, fills, strokes, opacity, and object names. Use paths for membranes, energy curves, ribbons, polymer chains, and abstract flow lines.
